cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 23-year-old presents to ED unwell with with abdominal pain and feeling generally unwell.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winterâ€™s formula predicts PaCO2 ~23.0 (Â±2); measured 23.2 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 134 âˆ’ (101 + 10) = 23 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old attends ED with deep rapid breathing, with reduced oral intake, and with abdominal pain.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winterâ€™s formula predicts PaCO2 ~24.5 (Â±2); measured 23.3 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 139 âˆ’ (104 + 11) = 24 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in COâ‚‚ on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old is brought to ED with known COPD, after several days of breathlessness, and with hypercapnic symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in COâ‚‚ on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with nausea, hyperventilation, and tinnitus after ingesting a large quantity of tablets.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (138 âˆ’ (106 + 11) = 21) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with reduced consciousness, shallow respirations, and pinpoint pupils.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.0; measured 26.4 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with longstanding breathlessness presents with increased sputum and fatigue.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~31.6; measured 30.6 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 24-year-old attends ED unwell with tachypnoeic, with persistent vomiting, and after missed insulin doses.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 30-year-old presents with rapid breathing, light-headedness, and perioral tingling after an acute stressful event.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.4; measured 21.2 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 35-year-old presents with weakness and dehydration after ongoing gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~45.6; measured 45.0 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes high anion gap metabolic acidosis due to lactate accumulation.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old is brought to ED with feeling generally unwell and with deep rapid breathing.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winterâ€™s formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (Â±2); measured 26.7 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 âˆ’ (96 + 12) = 30 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.clinical_stem: 31-year-old presents to ED unwell with tachypnoeic and with reduced oral intake.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winterâ€™s formula predicts PaCO2 ~29.0 (Â±2); measured 30.0 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 141 âˆ’ (98 + 14) = 29 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old attends ED with tachypnoeic and with rigors.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.clinical_stem: 27-year-old presents with nausea, hyperventilation, and tinnitus after ingesting a large quantity of tablets.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (140 âˆ’ (98 + 16) = 26) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.
